Mercedes E‑Class E200/E220 Diesel Engine Codes

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes E‑Class E200 / E220 diesel engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned E‑Class diesel engine typically costs £1,800–£3,800 supply only depending on engine generation (OM611, OM646 or OM651). A reconditioned engine typically costs £1,200–£2,200, with total installed cost around £1,800–£2,800. A new OEM engine replacement can cost £2,500–£4,000+, with labour pushing the total above £5,000. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,500. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your vehicle.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes E‑Class E200 / E220 diesel engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on which generation your E‑Class has. The OM651 (W212, 2009‑2016) is a 'very good engine if well maintained and looked after', but it has a well‑documented timing chain weakness. The OM646 (W211, 2002‑2009) is robust and features a Lanchester balancer shaft for smoothness. The OM611 (W210, 1998‑2002) is an early common‑rail diesel with a solid reputation. Regular oil changes (every 8,000‑10,000 miles) are essential for all three engines.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes E‑Class E200 / E220 diesel?

acceptedAnswer.text: On the OM651 (W212), the most common issue is timing chain wear – causing rattling on cold start. On the OM611 and OM646 (W210/W211), the most common issues are fuel injector problems (rough idle, hard starting) and timing chain wear. All three engines can suffer from EGR valve and swirl flap issues. The OM651 has a known timing chain weakness at around 90,000 miles.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an E‑Class diesel engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and condition. Against a tidy £3,000–£10,000 E‑Class, a £1,800–£3,800 recon is 20‑60% of the car's value – often worth it on well‑maintained examples. If the timing chain has snapped on an OM651, a full engine replacement may be necessary. Get a full diagnostic assessment before committing.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my E‑Class's engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The W210 (1998‑2002) uses the OM611.961 engine – a 2.2‑litre inline‑four. The W211 (2002‑2009) uses the OM646.951 (E200 CDI) or OM646.961 (E220 CDI) – a 2.2‑litre inline‑four. The W212 (2009‑2016) uses the OM651.911/.921/.951 – a 2.1‑litre inline‑four. The engine code is stamped on the engine block and listed on your V5C.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair an E‑Class diesel engine issue, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Most issues – OM651 timing chain wear (if caught early), fuel injector problems, EGR valve issues – are repairable. A timing chain and tensioner replacement on an OM651 is around £700 at a good independent specialist. However, if the timing chain has snapped on an OM651, a full engine replacement is usually more cost‑effective than a rebuild.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes E-Class E200 / E220 Diesel Engine Replacement Costs

rows.bestFor: OM611/OM646 no longer available new; OM651 limited availability

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the E-Class diesel pricing across generations. A reconditioned engine typically costs £1,200–£2,200, with total installed cost around £1,800–£2,800. A new OEM engine replacement can cost £2,500–£4,000+, with labour pushing the total above £5,000. The OM651 is at the upper end of the range. For reference, a timing chain and tensioner replacement on an OM651 is around £700 at a good independent specialist.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an E-Class diesel engine typically takes 10‑14 hours at £75‑£110/hr (independent Mercedes specialist). The OM651 is more tightly packaged than the earlier OM646 and OM611, adding some complexity. A timing chain replacement on the OM651 is considered "not a huge job to replace".

cta.label: → Compare E-Class diesel engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: OM651 Timing Chain Wear (W212, 2009–2016)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold start (especially noticeable when cold), metallic ticking, rattling persisting when warm, engine warning light, rough running. The timing chain is a known weak point on the OM651. A noisy chain at 160,000 miles is "asking for trouble if not sorted".
problems.typicalMileage: 90,000–160,000 miles. Many owners report noise around 90,000 miles.
problems.repairCost: £700–£1,500 [PLACEHOLDER] (timing chain and tensioner replacement — a known weak spot but "really isn't the end of the world compared to some cars issues").
problems.replacementCost: £2,200–£3,800 [PLACEHOLDER] (if chain snaps or jumps and damages the engine).

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattling on cold start, book an inspection immediately.

problems.recommendation: The OM651 timing chain is the defining weakness of the W212 E-Class diesel. The stop‑start system is often blamed for causing additional stress to the chain. A timing chain and tensioner replacement is around £700 at a good independent specialist. Excessive use of the Mercedes stop‑start system is usually blamed for this. If the chain breaks or becomes loose enough to jump on the sprockets, the engine will be damaged — "such damage that might need the head to come off to rectify and that's going to be very expensive". At the first sign of cold‑start rattle, have the timing system inspected immediately.

problems.title: OM611 / OM646 Fuel Injector Issues (W210 & W211)
problems.symptoms: Rough idle, hard starting, misfires, poor fuel economy, engine warning light, black smoke from exhaust, engine stalling.
problems.repairCost: £300–£800 [PLACEHOLDER] (injector cleaning or replacement — remanufactured Bosch injectors available from around £165.84).

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if rough running or hard starting occurs.

problems.recommendation: The OM611 and OM646 are known for fuel injector issues. The Bosch common‑rail system is generally reliable, but injectors can suffer from wear and clogging over time. Remanufactured Bosch exchange diesel injectors are available from around £165.84. If the engine runs rough or has starting issues, have the injectors tested for leak‑off. Regular use of quality diesel and occasional fuel system cleaner can help prevent injector issues.

problems.title: OM651 Injector & Fuel System Issues (W212)
problems.symptoms: Rough idle, misfires, hard starting, poor fuel economy, engine warning light, black smoke from exhaust.

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if rough running or hard starting occurs.

problems.recommendation: The OM651's fuel injectors can suffer from wear and clogging. Injector issues can cause rough running, hard starting, and poor fuel economy. Regular fuel filter changes and using high‑quality diesel are essential. If the engine runs rough or has starting issues, have the injectors tested for leak‑off.

problems.title: OM611 / OM646 Camshaft & Timing Chain Wear (W210 & W211)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise from the engine on start‑up, metallic ticking, loss of power, rough running, engine warning light.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattling on start‑up, book an inspection immediately.

problems.recommendation: The OM611 and OM646 timing chains can stretch over time, and the camshaft can wear. The chain typically requires replacement after 160,000‑200,000 km. Using the incorrect engine oil grade is a major cause of timing chain issues. At the first sign of rattling, have the timing system inspected immediately.

problems.title: OM651 EGR Valve & Swirl Flap Issues (W212)
problems.symptoms: Reduced power, rough idle, increased fuel consumption, engine warning light, black smoke from exhaust.

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon if symptoms develop.

problems.recommendation: The OM651's EGR valve can become clogged with soot over time. This can lead to reduced power, rough idle, and increased fuel consumption. Regular motorway driving and using high‑quality diesel can help reduce EGR clogging. Swirl flap issues in the intake manifold can also occur. If the engine warning light appears with EGR‑related codes, have the system diagnosed promptly.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Mercedes E-Class E200 / E220 Diesel — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Catch it early — a known weak spot but "not the end of the world"
Full engine replacement — head damage is likely
Catch it early — cheaper than engine replacement
Worth fixing — common and well‑understood repair
Full engine replacement often more cost‑effective

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Mercedes E-Class Diesel? Check These First
buyIf: * Full Mercedes service history with regular oil changes (every 8,000‑10,000 miles)
* W212 (OM651): Timing chain quiet — no rattling on cold start
* W211 (OM646): Smooth running — balancer shaft engines are "as smooth as a six‑cylinder"
* W210 (OM611): Injectors in good health — no hard starting or rough idle
* A well‑maintained example — the OM651 is a "very good engine if well maintained and looked after"
avoidIf: * W212 (OM651): Any sign of cold‑start rattle — timing chain warning
* W210 (OM611): Any sign of injector issues — hard starting or rough idle
* W211 (OM646): Any sign of timing chain rattle — the OM646 is robust but not immune
* Poor service history — these engines are intolerant of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The Mercedes E-Class E200 and E220 diesel models represent three generations of some of the finest diesel engines Mercedes ever produced. The OM611 in the W210 (1998‑2002) was an early common‑rail diesel with a solid reputation. The OM646 in the W211 (2002‑2009) featured a Lanchester balancer shaft, making it "as smooth as a six‑cylinder". The OM651 in the W212 (2009‑2016) is the most‑produced engine in Mercedes‑Benz history — a "very good engine if well maintained and looked after", but it has a well‑documented timing chain weakness. Buy on service history above all else — a well‑maintained E-Class diesel is a rewarding and reliable car; a neglected example can be expensive to repair.

cta.label: → Compare E-Class diesel engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes E-Class E200 / E220 diesel (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Cold Start — Any rattling? (timing chain warning — W212 OM651 especially)
2. Service History — Regular oil changes (every 8,000‑10,000 miles) — this is non‑negotiable
3. Injector Health — Any hard starting, rough idle, or misfires? (injector warning)
4. Timing Chain History — Has the chain been inspected or replaced? (especially OM651)
5. Running Quality — Any loss of power or hesitation? (EGR or swirl flap warning)
6. Oil Leaks — Check under the vehicle and around the engine for any signs of leakage
7. Oil Consumption — Does it use more than 1L per 1,000 miles?
8. MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures or any advisory notes
